titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Diane Torr Papers MSS.527
titlestmt.author: Rachel Mahre, Natalia Medero

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-05-12 16:36:47 UTC.

change.item: Updated to include materials integrated from accession number 2018.097 by Samantha Rowe. Edits by Samantha Rowe for compliance with DACS and ACM Required Elements for Archival Description.
Updated to create archival objects for films inspected by Media Preservation.
Updated by Rachel Mahre to process the collection.

physdesc.extent: in 61 manuscript boxes, 13 record cartons, 6 flat boxes, 4 CD boxes, 3 cassette boxes, 1 LP box, and 4 flat files.

did.abstract: Diane Torr (1948-2017) was an artist, writer, and educator known for her drag king performances, "Man for a Day" workshops on movement and gender expression, and artistic interrogations of conceptions of gender. Torr moved to New York in 1976, where she become involved in the city's Downtown arts scene and helped to establish the drag king culture both domestically and internationally. The Diane Torr Papers contain ephemera, administrative documents, and film and video recodings that document Torr's performances and exhibits; workshops, courses, and seminars; film and video work; written works and journals; community organizing and activism work; and personal life.
did.physloc: Materials are stored offsite and advance notice is required for use. Please request materials at least two business days prior to your research visit to coordinate access.
did.langmaterial: A majority of the materials are in English, with some press clippings and event ephemera in German, Dutch, Italian, French, Korean, Italian, Spanish, Basque, and Portuguese.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Donated by Martina Meijer, Diane Torr's daughter, in August 2017; the accession number associated with this gift is 2018.024. An additional accretion of materials was donated by Martina Meijer in August of 2018. The accession number associated with the accretion is 2018.097.

appraisal.head: Appraisal
appraisal.p: During processing in 2026, the following materials were deaccessioned from the collection:
One large plastic banner due to its degraded condition;
4 linear feet of paper materials, including ephemera duplicates, work by Torr's students, personal correspondence and photographs, and sensitive administrative and financial records;
5 linear feet of published texts, including newspapers, magazines, and books;
11 analog audiovisual recordings that were blank, commercial, or personal in content;
and 105 born-digital recordings that were commercial, duplicative, blank, or personal in content.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This collection is arranged into seven series, with Series II and III further arranged into subseries:
Series I. Performances and Exhibitions
Series II. Workshops and Teaching
Subseries II.A. Drag Workshops
Subseries II.B. Courses and Seminars
Series III. Film and Television
Subseries III.A. Film and Television Works
Subseries III.B. Film Festivals and Screenings
Series IV. Written Works
Series V. Community Organizing
Series VI. Press Coverage
Series VII. Personal Files

bioghist.head: Biographical Note
bioghist.p: Diane Torr (1948-2017) was an artist, writer, and educator known for her drag king performances, "Man for a Day" workshops on movement and gender expression, and artistic interrogations of conceptions of gender. She worked in multiple mediums, including dance, film, installation art, and performance art.

Torr studied Dance Education at Dartington College of Arts in England and moved to New York in 1976 following graduation, where she become involved in the city's Downtown arts scene. She regularly performed at the Mudd Club, Club 57, The Pyramid Club, MoMA PS1, Franklin Furnace, Performance Space 122, WOW Cafe, and La Mama Theater. She was an original member of the female art band DISBAND, along with Martha Wilson, Ingrid Sischy, Ilona Granet, and Donna Henes. Her performances critically examined gender and sexuality, and the performative nature of femininity and masculinity.

Torr's earliest dance performances were in 1977, and her first performance in drag was "Arousing Reconstructions" in 1981. Torr performed several international tours, including "Amoebic Evolution" and "Catastrophe and Beguilement" in the late 1980s, "Ready Aye Ready (A Standing Cock Has Nae Conscience)" in the early 1990s, "Drag Kings and Subjects" from the 1990s through the 2010's, and "Donald Does Dusty" in the 2000's. She organized the "Go Drag!" Berlin drag performance festival with Bridge Markland in 2002, and participated in numerous exhibitions at galleries such as the Glasgow Gallery of Modern Art, NYU's Grey Art Gallery, and the Institute of Contemporary Art in Boston.

Torr was an integral part of the "young kings" movement, establishing a scene of drag kings in 1980's New York City. This community of drag kings is the subject of the 2002 film Venus Boyz. Some of her most performed characters include Danny King ("Drag Kings and Subjects"), a "middle-aged, middle-American, middle-management guy" who "represents a stereotype of what it is to be a man"; Jack Spratt, a singer/songwriter Cockney character who is a fan of the Who; Hamish McAllister ("Ready Aye Ready"), an aficionado of the Scottish poet Robert Burns; and Charles Beresford, a gay man based on one of Torr's friends who passed away in 1992 from HIV/AIDS. Collaborators and contemporaries include Annie Sprinkle, Johnny Science, Bridge Markland, and Del La Grace Volcano. Torr was also involved in many community organizations, including Guerrilla Girls, PONY (Prostitutes of New York), FTM International, and F2M Fraternity.

She received her MFA from Bard College in 2004, and spent her later years in Scotland, where she was a visiting lecturer at Glasgow School of Art. In 2010, Torr published the book Sex, Drag, and Male Roles: Investigating Gender as Performance, which documents her career in the context of a wider history of female-to-male cross-dressing and performance. Diane Torr passed away in 2017.

phystech.head: Born-Digital Access Policies and Procedures
phystech.p: Advance notice is required for the use of computer records. Original physical digital media is restricted. An access terminal for born-digital materials in the collection is available by appointment for reading room viewing and listening only. Researchers may view an item's original container and/or carrier, but the physical carriers themselves are not available for use because of preservation concerns.

phystech.head: Audiovisual Access Policies and Procedures
phystech.p: Audiovisual materials have not been preserved and may not be available to researchers. Materials not yet digitized will need to have access copies made before they can be used. To request an access copy, or if you are unsure if an item has been digitized, please contact NYU Special Collections (special.collections@nyu.edu, 212-998-2596) with the collection name, collection number, and a description of the item(s) requested. A staff member will respond to you with further information.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Identification of item, date; Diane Torr Papers; MSS 527; box number; folder number or item identifier; Fales Library and Special Collections, New York University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: At the time of accessioning, materials were rehoused in archival boxes and folders, maintaining original order. Loose materials were housed in folders but not grouped or arranged in any particular way.

An accretion of materials was donated by Martina Meijer in August 2018 (Boxes 32-54). At the time of accessioning, materials were placed in acid-free folders and boxes. Original caption information, when available, was transcribed onto acid-free archival folders. Materials that were loose was foldered and titled. All materials were organized in the order the content was received.

In 2026, this collection was arranged by an archivist. Paper materials were placed in acid-free folders in manuscript boxes, oversized materials were foldered and housed appropriately according to their size, and audiovisual materials were placed in format-appropriate housing. Paper, audiovisual, and born-digital materials were arranged intellectually and their description was integrated.

During processing, many posters were flattened and treated for rips and tears by the Preservation Department. Some folded elements inside Torr's journals were flattened and foldered separately. Lanyards from film festivals were dismantled, and festival badges were placed together in photo sleeves. Two cracked VHS cartridges were replaced.

In 2025-2026, born-digital carriers containing data were forensically imaged, analyzed, and arranged in Forensic Toolkit. The born-digital audio and video recordings were arranged separately in directories on locally-mounted networked storage and intellectually integrated into appropriate series. New York University Libraries follow professional standards and best practices when imaging, ingesting, and processing born-digital material in order to maintain the integrity and authenticity of the content.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Diane Torr Papers (1907-2016) consist of materials created and collected by Diane Torr, documenting her career as a performance artist working in dance and drag king performance, a filmmaker, and an author. They also document her personal life, her involvement in community organizations, and the larger contexts of examinations and interrogations of gender identity and expression through artistic means.

The bulk of the materials in this collection document Torr's drag king performances and her "Man for a Day Workshops," including posters, photographs in analog and digital format, video recordings of performances on various formats, ephemera publicizing individual performances or festivals Torr participated in, and clippings of press coverage Torr received. In addition to her "Man for a Day" workshops, the collection documents Torr's teaching career, including syllabi, notes, ephemera, and recordings from university courses, movement workshops, primary school performances, and academic lectures.

Torr's film and video work, as well as her television appearances, are portrayed in analog and digital recordings, production files, and festival and screening ephemera. This collection also includes Torr's original writings on issues related to gender performance, as well as her notebooks, journals, and diaries, and research files on topics related to gender identity, drag performance, women's rights, and the LGBT community.

Torr's involvement in community organizations and political activism groups is portrayed in newsletters, bulletins, zines, ephemera, awards, meeting minutes and notes, correspondence, contact lists, and budget reports for groups including FTM International, PONY (Prostitutes of New York), and the Guerrilla Girls. Her personal life is also well documented in planners, address books, contact lists, correspondence, curricula vitae and biographical statements, and personal notes.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: This collection is protected by copyright and/or related rights. You are free to use materials in the collection in any way that is permitted by the copyright and related rights legislation that applies to your use. For other uses you need to obtain permission from the rights-holder(s).

controlaccess.subject: Cross-dressers
Drag community
Drag kings
Drag shows
Gay and lesbian film festivals -- 20th century
Gay and lesbian film festivals. -- 21st century
Gender bending
Gender identity
Gender identity in the theater
Lesbian theater
Performance art.
Performance art -- New York (N.Y)

physdesc.extent: in 14 manuscript boxes, 13 record cartons, 3 cassette boxes, 3 flat boxes, 1 LP box, and and 3 flat files.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged chronologically, with materials from the same event grouped together. Undated events are arranged alphabetically at the end of the list. Performance photographs were arranged into event files when possible, and are otherwise grouped together at the end of the series.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I. Performances and Exhibitions contains video recordings, paper documentation, and digital files related to Diane Torr's performances and exhibitions. Paper files for events include ephemera such as posters, postcards, pamphlets and flyers; planning documentation, such as correspondence, technical addendums, and performance contracts; photographs; press clippings and press releases; and performance setlists, cue sheets, artists statements, and scripts.

In addition to singular performances, this series contains documentation of Torr's interational tours, including "Amoebic Evolution" and "Catastrophe and Beguilement" in the late 1980s, "Ready Aye Ready (A Standing Cock Has Nae Conscience)" in the early 1990s, "Drag Kings and Subjects" from the 1990s through the 2010s, and "Donald Does Dusty" in the 2000s. The series also includes materials related to Torr's band Disband, the "Go Drag!" festival that she organized with Bridge Markland, and numerous exhibitions at galleries such as the Glasgow Gallery of Modern Art, NYU's Grey Art Gallery, and the Institute of Contemporary Art in Boston.

The materials in this series portray Torr's role in cultivating the "young kings" movement, when drag kings began to emerge in 1980s-1990s New York City. Her drag characters are highlighted in the materials, including Danny King ("Drag Kings and Subjects"), a "middle-aged, middle-American, middle-management guy" who "represents a stereotype of what it is to be a man"; Jack Spratt, a singer/songwriter Cockney fan of the Who; Hamish McAllister ("Ready Aye Ready"), an aficionado of the Scottish poet Robert Burns; and Charles Beresford, a gay man based on one of Torr's friends who passed away in 1992 from HIV/AIDS. Collaborators and contemporaries represented in this series include Annie Sprinkle, Bridge Markland, Johnny Science, and Del La Grace Volcano. Venues that Torr performed at most often include WOW Cafe, Performance Space 122, Franklin Furnace, Club Casanova, the Pyramid, and Club 57.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Amoebic Evolution" and "Catastrophe and Beguilement" are two works that Torr often performed together, along with other works including "Girl in Trouble," "I.V. Mermaid," "Go-Go World," and "Pssssst." Records portraying Torr's international tour of these works from 1986-1989 have been grouped together, and when possible the works represented in each file are described in the record title.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: "Drag Kings and Subjects" was often performed alongside Torr's "Man for a Day" workshops. For materials pertaining to workshops taught at these events, see Subseries II.A.

physdesc.extent: in 8 manuscript boxes, 8 record cartons, 2 cassette boxes, 2 flat boxes, and 3 flat files.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This subseries is arranged chronologically with materials from the same workshop grouped together.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Subseries II.A Drag Workshops contains materials related to Diane Torr's "Man for a Day" workshops, as well as other workshops and lectures on the topics of drag and cross-dressing. Torr toured "Man for a Day" workshops internationally on college campuses, on performance stages, and in festival lineups starting in 1992, often alongside the performance "Drag Kings and Subjects." Materials referencing workshops may also be found in the "Drag Kings and Subjects" files in Series I. Records in this subseries include ephemera, planning correspondence, press clippings, press releases, workshop notes and descriptions, video recordings of workshops, testimonials, surveys, sign-in books, and photographs.

did.unittitle: "The Politics and Practice of Cross-Dressing" / "King for a Night," Artists Space

Workshop at Schule für Tanz und Theater

Workshop at West Yorkshire Playhouse, Leeds

"Drag Kings of the World" Workshop Series
"Public Women Private Concerns" Workshop, Sushi, San Diego

Flyer Mock-Up for Johnny Science Workshop

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This subseries is arranged chronologically with materials from the same teaching engagement grouped together.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Subseries II.B Courses and Seminars contains materials related to Diane Torr's teaching career. In addition to "Man for a Day" workshops, Torr taught university courses, seminars, lectures, and workshops on dance, performance art, martial arts, and gender studies. In particular, she studied and taught the Release Technique and Contact Improvisation dance methods. She also taught performance art to school children. Materials in this series include correspondence, job applications, syllabi, course reading materials, notes, flyers, calendars and programs, and video recordings.

physdesc.extent: in 12 record cartons, 5 manuscript boxes, 2 cassette boxes, 2 flat boxes, and 2 flat files.

did.unittitle: Subseries III.A. Film and Television Works

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This subseries is arranged alphabetically by the title of the work, with materials from the same work grouped together.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Subseries III.A. Film and Television Works contains audiovisual recordings, ephemera, and production files for Torr's film and video works, as well as her television appearances. Production files may contain a variety of documents, including film ephemera such as posters and flyers; funding applications; correspondence; production contracts; tape logs; receipts and invoices; and screening schedules. This series may also include works by Torr's collaborators and contemporaries.

bioghist.p: This film was made in collaboration with students during Torr's residency at the Glasgow School of Art in the Spring of 2003.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Subseries III.B. Film Festivals and Screenings contains ephemera from film festivals attended by Torr, including posters, flyers programs, schedules, badges, applications for festivals, and related correspondence.

physdesc.extent: in 17 manuscript boxes and 1 flat box.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged alphabetically. Torr's journals are grouped together and arranged chronologically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series IV. Written Works includes both published and unpublished written works by Diane Torr, as well as research files that she maintained on a variety of topics. A bulk of the series is comprised of Torr's journals, maintained from 1973-2016. Torr published the book Sex, Drag, and Male Roles: Investigating Gender as Performance in 2010. She also contributed to several anthologies and works by other authors, including Dick for a Day, edited by Fiona Giles (1997); Sex Tips: Advice from Women Experts Around the World by Jo-Anne Baker (1999); and Aroused: A Collection of Erotic Writing, edited by Karen Finley (2001). Torr also wrote shorter pieces on a variety of personal and professional topics, such as dance, performance, birth, death, eroticism, aging, and gender. This series also includes correspondence with literary publishers, and with authors during the writing of Female Masculinity by Jack Halberstam (1998) and Memories of the Revolution: The First Ten Years of the WOW Cafe Theater by Holly Hughes et al (2015).

did.unittitle: Arts and Humanities Research Council -- Fellowship applications

physdesc.extent: in 6 manuscript boxes, 2 record cartons, and 2 flat boxes.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged chronologically with materials related to the same organization or topic grouped together.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series V. Community Organizing contains materials related to Torr's activities in community organizations and political advocacy groups, including newsletters, bulletins, zines, ephemera, awards, meeting minutes and notes, correspondence, contact lists, and budget reports. Organizations particularly well-represented in this series include FTM International, PONY (Prostitutes of New York), and the Guerrilla Girls. The series also contains artistic community projects such as drag king calendars in 1994 and 2004, and Annie Sprinkle's "Post-Modern Pin-ups: Pleasure Activist Playing Cards" (1995); community archiving projects such as Franklin Furnace's "Archives of the Avant-Garde" and Glasgow Women's Library "Documenting 109" Project; and organizing documents for community art spaces, such as Chez Bushwick and the Work Room Studio.

did.unittitle: Event files -- proposals, contracts, and ephemera
Video recording -- "Guerrilla Girls Reveal Secret Identities!"
Self-Health for Queensland Workers in the Sex Industry (SQWISI)
The National Association of Artists' Organizations (NAAO)

Glasgow Gallery of Modern Art -- Censorship boycott and petition

physdesc.extent: in 4 manuscript boxes, 4 record cartons, and 3 flat boxes.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged chronologically with professional portraits grouped together at the beginning of the list.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series VI. Press Coverage contains press clippings about Diane Torr's career. Clippings range from 1981 to 2014 and are published by a wide variety of international newspapers and magazines. Wherever possible, clippings about particular performances have been arranged into the performance files in Series I; therefore, this series contains clippings that pertain to Torr's career and influence more broadly. This series also contains professional portraits of Torr, as well as written and audiovisual interviews.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These photographs feature Diane Torr both in and out of drag. The photos in drag are grouped together by character, including Hamish McAllister, Charles Beresford, Jack Spratt, and Danny King.

physdesc.extent: in 14 manuscript boxes, 6 record cartons, 3 cassette boxes, and 2 flat boxes.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged chronologically with materials of the same content type grouped together.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series VII. Personal Files contains a wide variety of materials related to Torr's personal life, including planners, address books, contact lists, correspondence, and curricula vitae and biographical statements. Materials related to Torr's education include records from her high school education; her dance educaton at Dartington College of Arts; and other trainings and certifications such as shorthand, counseling and psychotherapy, aikido, and shiatsu. This series also includes performance and exhibition ephemera produced by other artists and contemporaries, collected by Torr as she worked and traveled; books with inscriptions, given to Torr as gifts from authors and artists that she was in community with; notes related to Torr's personal studies, such as learning languages, shiatsu, acting, anatomy, yoga, and acupuncture; audiovisual recordings depicting Torr's travels; fiiles and mockup designs for her website; and photographs and recordings used as reference for Torr's teaching and performance work.

did.unittitle: "When a Fellow is Twenty One, as introduced by Hetty King" -- Sheet music
